answers.question: List of scenarios
answer: Orderly (1.5°C-aligned) — NGFS orderly 'net-zero 2050' / IPCC SSP1-1.9 / NIWA RCP 2.6 / CCC 'Tailwinds'
Disorderly (other-aligned) — NGFS disorderly 'delayed 2°C' / IPCC SSP1-2.6 / NIWA RCP 2.6 / CCC 'Headwinds'
Hot-house world (>3°C-aligned) — NGFS hot-house 'current policies' / IPCC SSP5-8.5 / NIWA RCP 8.5 / CCC current policies
answers.evidence: "Orderly (1.5°C-aligned) … Disorderly (other-aligned) … Hot-house world (>3°C-aligned) … Global physical and socioeconomic parameters: IPCC SSP1-1.9 / IPCC SSP1-2.6 / IPCC SSP5-8.5 … New Zealand physical parameters: eg. NIWA RCP 2.6 / NIWA RCP 2.6 / NIWA RCP 8.5 … Global transition and energy use: NGFS orderly 'net-zero 2050' scenario / NGFS disorderly 'delayed 2°C' / NGFS hot-house 'current policies' … New Zealand transition pathways: Climate Change Commission (CCC) 'Tailwinds' / CCC 'Headwinds' / CCC current policies"
answers.location_hint: Architecture of the Bank's Three Entity-Specific Scenarios, p.28–29
answers.notes: The Bank developed three entity-specific scenarios by blending IPCC SSP, NGFS, NIWA RCP, and CCC pathways together.

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Three temperature outcomes are described: ~1.4°C (Orderly/1.5°C-aligned), ~1.8°C (Disorderly), and ~4.4°C (Hot-house world). The Bank notes that the 1.5°C scenario has a projected temperature of 1.4°C due to the limited number of available IPCC scenarios. The overarching goal is to limit warming to 1.5°C (with likely limited/no overshoot) by end of century.
answers.evidence: "Approximate warming at 2100: ~1.4°C [Orderly] / ~1.8°C [Disorderly] / ~4.4°C [Hot-house world] … It should be noted that, due to the limited number of scenarios available and the nature of temperature projections being based on probabilities and ranges, this has led to our 1.5°C scenario having a projected temperature in 2100 of 1.4°C (which is the closest available IPCC scenario to 1.5°C)."
answers.location_hint: Architecture of the Bank's Three Entity-Specific Scenarios, p.28–29
answers.notes: No explicit temperature overshoot pathway is described beyond the note about 1.4°C being the closest available to 1.5°C. The transition planning section mentions "1.5°C (with a likely limited/no overshoot)".

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: Multiple scenario providers are explicitly named: IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change), NGFS (Network for Greening the Financial System), NIWA (National Institute of Water and Atmospheric Research), and CCC (Climate Change Commission).
answers.evidence: "For physical data, we have used the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) Shared Socioeconomic Pathway (SSP) scenarios. For transition data, we have used the Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) scenarios." … "New Zealand physical parameters: eg. NIWA RCP 2.6 … New Zealand transition pathways: Climate Change Commission (CCC) 'Tailwinds' / CCC 'Headwinds' / CCC current policies"

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: "Global physical and socioeconomic parameters: IPCC SSP1-1.9 / IPCC SSP1-2.6 / IPCC SSP5-8.5 … New Zealand physical parameters: eg. NIWA RCP 2.6 / NIWA RCP 2.6 / NIWA RCP 8.5"
answers.location_hint: Architecture of the Bank's Three Entity-Specific Scenarios, p.28–29
answers.notes: The document explicitly lists both SSP identifiers (IPCC SSP1-1.9, SSP1-2.6, SSP5-8.5) and RCP identifiers (NIWA RCP 2.6, RCP 8.5) used together within the same scenario framework. SSPs and RCPs are from different scenario generations and are not from the same internally consistent family.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: "Therefore, our own scenarios have been developed, as described below, through blending recognised and coherent scenarios on physical and transition risks together, incorporating relevant datapoints and elements. Narrative detail has then been added based on those datapoints and relevant information for our business." … "Data sources: Physical: NIWA regional, high-intensity rainfall and sea-level rise projections and IPCC WGI Interactive atlas regional synthesis. Economic: GCAM 5.3+ NGFS World Downscaled and GCAM 6.0 NGFS (GDP only)."
answers.notes: The Bank explicitly developed its own entity-specific scenarios by blending IPCC SSP, NGFS, NIWA (downscaled regional), and CCC pathways, and added narrative detail tailored to its agribusiness portfolio in New Zealand. The use of "NIWA regional" projections and "NGFS World Downscaled" data confirms localisation/downscaling.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: "The scenario development analysis and results involved engagement and governance at a number of levels of the business: Project Leads (CSO and Senior Risk Advisor) – led and coordinated work and material creation. Subject matter experts (SMEs) – provided specialist input as required. A steering Committee … Executive committee approvals … Board – Reviewed, discussed and approved scenario architecture. The Bank worked with consultancy Deloitte to assist with scenario development and assess the related climate change risk and opportunities."
answers.notes: Scenario development involved internal governance layers and external consultancy (Deloitte), but there is no mention of participatory, co-created, deliberative, or stakeholder co-design processes with external parties such as customers, communities, or civil society.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.answer: No explicit standalone baseline/reference scenario is described. However, the NGFS "current policies" scenario (used as the Hot-house world scenario) and the CCC "current policies" pathway serve as a de facto current-policy reference point. The heatmap methodology also references a "worst-case scenario" as the basis for physical risk identification.
answers.evidence: "Hot-house world scenarios assume that some climate policies are implemented in some jurisdictions but global efforts are insufficient to halt significant global warming." … "NGFS hot-house 'current policies'" … "CCC current policies" … "To identify exposures, we have chosen to rely on a worst-case scenario for climate change using various trajectories for Greenhouse Gas concentrations spanning a period up to 2100."
answers.notes: No scenario is explicitly labelled as a "baseline" or "reference" scenario against which others are compared. The "current policies" scenario functions as a high-physical-risk reference, and the orderly scenario aligns with the Bank's Paris commitments. The heatmap methodology uses a worst-case scenario for physical risk identification.

answers.question: Model used
answers.evidence: "Data sources: Physical: NIWA regional, high-intensity rainfall and sea-level rise projections and IPCC WGI Interactive atlas regional synthesis. Economic: GCAM 5.3+ NGFS World Downscaled and GCAM 6.0 NGFS (GDP only)."
answers.notes: GCAM (Global Change Analysis Model) is the integrated assessment model used for economic/transition data. NIWA regional projections and IPCC WGI Interactive Atlas are used for physical data. No macroeconomic models (e.g. NiGEM) are named.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: "Alongside Scenario Analysis, the Bank is also introducing the use of Rabobank-developed heatmaps to quantify the physical impacts to the Bank's portfolio from key acute events, including cyclones, windstorms, riverine flooding, coastal flooding, heavy precipitation (or pluvial flooding), drought, wildfires and extreme heat." … "This analysis process was qualitative rather than quantitative. This has the advantage of removing the need for complicated quantitative modelling and some of the limitations that approach presents such as a failure to accurately measure all impacts, especially those from acute events."
answers.location_hint: Heatmaps section, p.34–35; Risk and Opportunity Identification, p.33
answers.notes: Acute physical risks (cyclones, floods, wildfires, etc.) are explicitly included in the heatmap analysis to quantify portfolio impacts. The scenario analysis itself is qualitative, but the heatmaps are designed to quantify acute event impacts. Real-world acute events (Auckland floods, Cyclone Gabrielle) are also discussed in the Current Impacts section.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answers.evidence: "Global temperature … Intense rainfall days … Hot days … Frost days … Sea-level rise (with vertical land move) … Global GHG emissions … Global GDP … Global population … Global oil price … Global agricultural demand … Global CCS use … Global carbon price"
answers.notes: These variables are explicitly listed in the scenario datapoint tables for each of the three scenarios.

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: "Global physical and socioeconomic parameters: IPCC SSP1-1.9 / IPCC SSP1-2.6 / IPCC SSP5-8.5 … New Zealand physical parameters: eg. NIWA RCP 2.6 … Data sources: Physical: NIWA regional, high-intensity rainfall and sea-level rise projections and IPCC WGI Interactive atlas regional synthesis. Economic: GCAM 5.3+ NGFS World Downscaled and GCAM 6.0 NGFS (GDP only)."
answers.notes: The scenarios use both global-level data (IPCC SSPs, NGFS/GCAM) and regionally downscaled data for New Zealand (NIWA regional projections, NGFS World Downscaled). The orderly scenario notes that downscaled data was not available for some physical variables under that scenario.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Short term: now–2030; Medium term: 2030–2050; Long term: 2050–2100. Rationales are explicitly provided for each timeframe.
answers.evidence: "Short term: now – 2030 – to align with the existing 5-year horizons in strategic planning, Medium Term Planning (MTP), Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process (ICAAP) and Funding Plan. During 2023, MTP horizon was extended to 2030 for asset growth by sector to align with Sector x Country Plans. Medium term: 2030–2050 – to reflect the contractual maturity profile of the Bank's loan book. Long term: 2050–2100 – recognise the intergenerational nature of farming and growing in New Zealand, ensure understanding of the longer-term implications of climate impacts and guard against the historical trend of improved science leading to impacts being brought forward."
answers.location_hint: Summary of Scenario Time Horizons and Risk Profiles, p.28–29
answers.notes: Justification is explicitly provided for all three timeframes. The document also notes these timeframes align with Rabobank group horizons (2020–2030, 2030–2040, 2040–2050) used in heatmaps and stress tests.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.evidence: "In developing our scenarios, the XRB's recommendation to use sectoral scenarios where advisable was considered both for efficiency and to aid comparability. As the only specialised agribusiness-focused bank operating in New Zealand, two sets of sector scenarios were relevant to us – the New Zealand Banking Association as well as Aotearoa Circle's Agriculture Sector Climate Change Scenarios. Neither of these provided us with the scenarios that best described plausible futures for us or in our view challenged our business sufficiently. The Bank also sought to align our climate scenarios with Rabobank to facilitate better reporting and analysis at a Rabobank-wide level. Therefore, our own scenarios have been developed, as described below, through blending recognised and coherent scenarios on physical and transition risks together, incorporating relevant datapoints and elements."
answers.location_hint: Assessing Impacts and Developing Business Resilience Through Climate-Related Scenarios, p.27
answers.notes: The Bank explicitly explains why it did not use available sector scenarios (NZ Banking Association and Aotearoa Circle) and why it chose to blend IPCC SSP and NGFS scenarios — to better describe plausible futures, challenge the business sufficiently, and align with Rabobank group reporting.
